an3.123:1.1: On one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Kusināra in the Baliharaṇa [Oblation-taking] forest grove.
an3.123:1.4: “Yes, lord,” the monks responded to him.
an3.123:2.1: “There is the case where a monk lives in dependence on a certain town or village.
an3.123:2.2: A householder or householder’s son, approaching him, invites him for the next day’s meal.
an3.123:2.3: If he wants to, he acquiesces.
an3.123:2.4: When the night has passed, early in the morning, he adjusts his under robe and—carrying his bowl & robes—goes to the residence of that householder or householder’s son. On arrival, he sits down on a seat laid out.
an3.123:2.5: The householder or householder’s son, with his own hand, serves & satisfies the monk with exquisite staple & non-staple food.
an3.123:3.1: The thought occurs to the monk,
an3.123:3.2: ‘How good it is that this householder or householder’s son serves & satisfies me with exquisite staple & non-staple food!’
an3.123:3.3: And the thought also occurs to him,
an3.123:3.4: ‘O, may this householder or householder’s son in the future serve & satisfy me with this sort of exquisite staple & non-staple food!’
an3.123:3.5: He consumes that almsfood attached to it, infatuated, guilty, not seeing the drawbacks (of attachment to it), and not discerning the escape from them.
an3.123:3.6: With regard to it, he thinks thoughts of sensuality, thoughts of ill will, thoughts of harmfulness.
an3.123:3.7: What is given to a monk of this sort, I tell you, is not of great fruit.
an3.123:4.1: But there is the case where a monk lives in dependence on a certain town or village.
an3.123:4.2: A householder or householder’s son, approaching him, invites him for the next day’s meal.
an3.123:4.3: If he wants to, he acquiesces.
an3.123:4.4: When the night has passed, early in the morning, he adjusts his under robe and—carrying his bowl & robes—goes to the residence of that householder or householder’s son. On arrival, he sits down on a seat laid out.
an3.123:4.5: The householder or householder’s son, with his own hand, serves & satisfies the monk with exquisite staple & non-staple food.
an3.123:5.1: The thought doesn’t occur to the monk,
an3.123:5.2: ‘How good it is that this householder or householder’s son serves & satisfies me with exquisite staple & non-staple food!’
an3.123:5.3: And the thought also doesn’t occur to him,
an3.123:5.4: ‘O, may this householder or householder’s son in the future serve & satisfy me with this sort of exquisite staple & non-staple food!’
an3.123:5.5: He consumes that almsfood unattached to it, uninfatuated, guiltless, seeing the drawbacks (of attachment to it), and discerning the escape from them.
an3.123:5.6: With regard to it, he thinks thoughts of renunciation, thoughts of non-ill will, thoughts of harmlessness.
an3.123:5.7: What is given to a monk of this sort, I tell you, is of great fruit.
